title: About Me
text:
I view life as one long learning experience. I love gathering knowledge from all disparate fields of study, ranging from engineering to political science. As part of this, I cannot help but focus on uncovering the mechanisms that form our world before our very eyes - politics. My aim is to become a researcher and professor of political science, incorporating data analytics with all its beauty to such a philosophical science.



Bachelors - Political Science, received 2024 from Montclair State University.
Masters - Data Analytics, Expected 2026 from University of Massachusetts.



2025 - Current: Office Administrator - Green Collar LLC
2024 - 2025: Research Assistant - APPRISE INC
2023 - 2024: Research Assistant - Montclair State University
2018 - 2024: Shift Leader - Qdoba

title: HomePopStateCode
text:

✔ purrr     1.1.0

[[1]]
 [1] "lubridate" "forcats"   "stringr"   "dplyr"     "purrr"     "readr"    
 [7] "tidyr"     "tibble"    "ggplot2"   "tidyverse" "stats"     "graphics" 
[13] "grDevices" "utils"     "datasets"  "methods"   "base"     

[[2]]
 [1] "lubridate" "forcats"   "stringr"   "dplyr"     "purrr"     "readr"    
 [7] "tidyr"     "tibble"    "ggplot2"   "tidyverse" "stats"     "graphics" 
[13] "grDevices" "utils"     "datasets"  "methods"   "base"     

[[3]]
 [1] "plotly"    "lubridate" "forcats"   "stringr"   "dplyr"     "purrr"    
 [7] "readr"     "tidyr"     "tibble"    "ggplot2"   "tidyverse" "stats"    
[13] "graphics"  "grDevices" "utils"     "datasets"  "methods"   "base"     

[[4]]
 [1] "usmap"     "plotly"    "lubridate" "forcats"   "stringr"   "dplyr"    
 [7] "purrr"     "readr"     "tidyr"     "tibble"    "ggplot2"   "tidyverse"
[13] "stats"     "graphics"  "grDevices" "utils"     "datasets"  "methods"  
[19] "base"     



HomeStateUse <- HomeState |>
  mutate(total = rowSums(across(4:11), na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  mutate(BedPer = total/HPop,
         region = tolower(State))

states_map <- map_data("state")
states_map <- states_map |>
  left_join(HomeStateUse, by = "region")


# {{< embed Data&Code/HomePopStateCode.qmd#ColGraph >}} -To call in other document

ggplot(HomeStateUse, aes(x = reorder(State, BedPer), y = BedPer, fill = BedPer > 1)) +
  geom_col(width = .6) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "blue", "FALSE" = "red"),
    labels = c("FALSE" = "<= 1", "TRUE" = "> 1"),
    guide = FALSE  # hides the legend; remove if you want legend
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Individual Emergency Shelter Beds per Homeless Individual by State (2024)",
    x = "State",
    y = "Beds per Homeless Individual"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)  # slant y-axis labels
  )

Warning: The `guide` argument in `scale_*()` cannot be `FALSE`. This was deprecated in
ggplot2 3.3.4.
ℹ Please use "none" instead.










ggplotly(
  ggplot(states_map, aes(
    x = long,
    y = lat,
    group = group,
    fill = BedPer,
    text = paste0(
      "State: ", tools::toTitleCase(region),
      "<br>Emergency Shelter Beds per Homeless Individual: ",
      round(BedPer, 2)
    )
  )) +
    geom_polygon(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
    coord_fixed(1.3) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(
      name = "Beds per\nHomeless Individual",
      low = "#570C18",
      
      high = "#000087",
      midpoint = 1,
      labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.01)
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "Shelter Bed Availability by State",
      subtitle = "Red = shortage (< 1), Blue = surplus (> 1)"
    ) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(
      legend.position = "right"
    ),
  tooltip = "text"
)

title: Welcome to some of my data visualization projects
text:
Review of Homelessness 2024
When looking at aggregate numbers state wide interesting patterns appear. All data is from the [Hud Exchange] and World Population News. [1][2]

Individual Emergency Shelter Beds Per Homeless Individual

title: Projects
text: As part of a white paper published by Montclair State University, two of my peers and I worked in close collaboration with the Paterson City Mayor’s Office to identify and develop solutions to an ongoing crisis in local emergency shelters. Local state law stipulates that the average length of stay in an emergency shelter must remain below a set number of days, but local shelters were not in compliance. We found that, by utilizing niche techniques ranging from merging Continuum of Cares to modifying funding schemes, local emergency shelter conditions could be brought into compliance with New Jersey state law.

title: Projects
text: As part of a white paper published by Montclair State University, two of my peers and I worked in close collaboration with the Paterson City Mayor’s Office to identify and develop solutions to an ongoing crisis in local emergency shelters. Local state law stipulates that the average length of stay in an emergency shelter must remain below a set number of days, but local shelters were not in compliance. We found that, by utilizing niche techniques ranging from merging Continuum of Cares to modifying funding schemes, local emergency shelter conditions could be brought into compliance with New Jersey state law.

title: Projects
section: An Exploratory Study of End-of-Life Planning Among Unsheltered and Unhoused Individuals
text:
An Exploratory Study of End-of-Life Planning Among Unsheltered and Unhoused Individuals
This project aims to examine how unsheltered and unhoused individuals engage in end-of-life (EoL) planning, an area that remains largely overlooked in homelessness and public health research. By drawing on survey data and semi-structured interviews, the study will explore how shelter status, geographic mobility, and access to medical and legal resources shape attitudes toward and engagement with EoL planning. By distinguishing between unsheltered individuals and those who are unhoused but mobile (e.g., living in vehicles), the research aims to highlight how mobility and fragmented service systems can disrupt continuity of care and planning at life’s end. It is my hope the findings will inform more humane and effective policies within Continuums of Care, emphasizing the importance of aligning end-of-life decisions with individual wishes rather than structural constraints.
